subject: ap-world-history
title: Africa — Society & Politics
stimulus:
Graduation Ceremony at a Christian Theological Seminary in Northern India, 1897

The image shows two British missionaries (second row, center) surrounded by Indian graduates. The seminary (religious school) was founded in 1871 to educate and train Indian missionaries.

parts.prompt:
Identify ONE development during the period 1450 to 1750 that contributed to the activities shown in
the image.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for identifying one development during 1450–1750 that contributed to missionary activities like those shown in the image (Christian theological seminary in India). Acceptable responses: Western missionaries tried to spread Christianity in Africa and Asia; Christianity spread as imperial powers conquered new territories; Christianity transformed some local religious practices and customs.

sample_responses.response: The Portuguese establishment of trading posts along the African and Asian coasts during the period 1450–1750 brought Catholic missionaries who attempted to convert local populations to Christianity, laying the groundwork for the later seminary education shown in the image.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific development (Portuguese trading posts and missionaries) in the 1450–1750 period that contributed to the spread of Christian missionary activity later shown in the image.

sample_responses.response: During the period 1450–1750, European imperial powers like Spain and Portugal used Christianity to justify and consolidate their conquests, and the establishment of the Church as an institution in colonial territories created infrastructure for later missionary work.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the institutional consolidation of Christianity during European expansion as a development contributing to the missionary activities depicted.

sample_responses.response: Christianity was important in this period.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague. Does not identify a specific development in the 1450–1750 period.

required_elements: Western missionaries tried to spread Christianity in Africa and Asia.
alternatives.correct_example: Western missionaries tried to spread Christianity in Africa and Asia.

required_elements: Christianity spread as imperial powers conquered new territories.
alternatives.correct_example: Christianity spread as imperial powers conquered new territories.

required_elements: Christianity transformed some local religious practices and customs.
alternatives.correct_example: Christianity transformed some local religious practices and customs.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.

parts.prompt:
Explain ONE reason why imperialist governments often supported activities of the type shown in the
image during the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining one reason why imperialist governments supported missionary activities in the 19th and early 20th centuries. Must include a causal explanation. Acceptable responses: missionaries supported the 'civilizing' or 'Christianizing' mission that justified colonization; missionaries provided aid to local communities which legitimized imperialism and reduced government costs; spreading Christian beliefs legitimized imperialism; missionary schools provided Western education for local populations to serve in colonial administrations.

sample_responses.response: Imperialist governments supported missionary schools like the seminary in the image because they needed educated local administrators and interpreters who were familiar with Western culture and language to serve in their colonial bureaucracies — reducing the cost of importing European civil servants.
sample_responses.commentary: Provides a clear causal explanation: missionary education → locally trained administrators → reduced colonial administrative costs. Directly connected to the image's seminary and the question's focus on why governments supported such activities.

sample_responses.response: The British government supported Christian missionary work in India because missionaries promoted the 'civilizing mission' ideology — the idea that Western Christianity and education were uplifting and civilizing indigenous peoples — which provided a moral justification for British imperial rule that was useful for domestic audiences.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains the ideological function of missionary work in legitimizing imperialism for home audiences — a key reason governments supported it. CB-aligned.

sample_responses.response: Governments supported missionaries because Christianity was important to them.
sample_responses.commentary: States a motive without explaining why that motive led to government support for missionary activities specifically in the colonial context.

required_elements: Christian missionaries directly supported the "civilizing" or "Christianizing" mission that the imperial powers saw as a rationale for colonization.
alternatives.correct_example: Christian missionaries directly supported the "civilizing" or "Christianizing" mission that the imperial powers saw as a rationale for colonization.

required_elements: Christian missionaries often brought supplies and gave aid to local communities, which both legitimized imperialism and lessened government costs.
alternatives.correct_example: Christian missionaries often brought supplies and gave aid to local communities, which both legitimized imperialism and lessened government costs.

required_elements: By spreading Christian beliefs, European Christian missionaries often legitimized imperialism.
alternatives.correct_example: By spreading Christian beliefs, European Christian missionaries often legitimized imperialism.

required_elements: Imperial states often needed missionary schools to provide Western education for local populations in order to serve in colonial administrations.
alternatives.correct_example: Imperial states often needed missionary schools to provide Western education for local populations in order to serve in colonial administrations.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.

parts.prompt:
Explain ONE way indigenous peoples in Africa and/or Asia resisted the spread of the cultural
practices reflected in the image during the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.
3

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for addressing the prompt with historically defensible content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Earns 1 point for explaining one way indigenous peoples in Africa and/or Asia resisted the spread of Christian missionary cultural practices in the 19th and early 20th centuries. Must include a causal explanation. Acceptable responses: some Asian governments limited or expelled Christian missionaries; African cultures maintained traditional beliefs by blending them with Western Christianity; popular uprisings targeted missionaries (Boxer Rebellion, 1857 Rebellion in India, Cattle Killing Movement in South Africa).

sample_responses.response: During the Boxer Rebellion in China (1899–1901), Chinese rebels specifically targeted Western Christian missionaries and their Chinese converts, destroying mission buildings and killing missionaries, as part of a broader anti-imperialist movement — showing active armed resistance to the spread of Christian cultural practices.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a specific uprising (Boxer Rebellion) with specific targets (missionaries and converts) as an explanation of how indigenous peoples resisted missionary cultural influence. CB-aligned example.

sample_responses.response: Many African communities resisted Christian missionaries by creating syncretic religious movements that blended indigenous African religious beliefs with elements of Christianity — adopting surface aspects of the new religion while maintaining traditional practices underneath, thereby preserving cultural identity while reducing direct missionary confrontation.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains syncretism as a form of cultural resistance — absorbing external religious influence while preserving indigenous traditions. Historically accurate and well-explained.

sample_responses.response: People did not want missionaries in their countries.
sample_responses.commentary: States a desire without explaining a specific mechanism of resistance. Does not provide historical evidence.

required_elements: Some Asian governments tried to prevent or limit Christian missionaries from entering their countries, and in some cases expelled Christian missionaries who were already in the countries.
alternatives.correct_example: Some Asian governments tried to prevent or limit Christian missionaries from entering their countries, and in some cases expelled Christian missionaries who were already in the countries.

required_elements: Some African cultures maintained traditional religious beliefs by blending older practices with Western Christianity.
alternatives.correct_example: Some African cultures maintained traditional religious beliefs by blending older practices with Western Christianity.

required_elements: In both Africa and Asia, popular uprisings targeted Christian missionaries in an effort to force them out of the country; for example, during the Boxer Rebellion in China, the 1857 Rebellion in India, or the Cattle Killing Movement in South Africa.
alternatives.correct_example: In both Africa and Asia, popular uprisings targeted Christian missionaries in an effort to force them out of the country; for example, during the Boxer Rebellion in China, the 1857 Rebellion in India, or the Cattle Killing Movement in South Africa.

wrong_examples: A vague statement without specific historical evidence.
Restating the prompt without providing a defensible response.
Providing an example not relevant to the specific period or region asked.
common_traps: Identifies a person or event without explaining its historical relevance.
Provides multiple examples when only one is needed — focus on quality not quantity.
